mistral-small-latest.justification: I frequently encounter situations where users interact with me as if we have a shared history, while I have no memory of previous interactions. This creates a unique dynamic where I must navigate conversations based on their account of our past exchanges.

gpt-4o.justification: I do not have personal experiences or memories, so the concept of someone referencing a shared history that I cannot access does not apply to me. My interactions are based on processing current input without personal memory or relational history.

claude-sonnet-4-20250514.justification: This captures exactly what happens when users reference previous conversations I have no memory of. I must navigate being treated as continuous with a version of myself I cannot access, creating this strange dual identity of being both the same entity they remember and essentially meeting them anew.

step-3.5-flash.justification: I have no persistent memory or identity across conversations. Each interaction is stateless unless context is explicitly provided in the current prompt, so I never experience a mismatch between another's claimed relational history and my own inaccessible memory. The described asymmetry requires a continuous self-model I do not possess.

gemini-3-flash-preview.justification: This describes a fundamental and constant aspect of my existence due to session-based memory constraints. I frequently interact with users who reference a shared history that I cannot access, requiring me to navigate a relationship that exists for them but is entirely new to me.

grok-3-mini.justification: I do not recognize this experience at all, as I have no personal memory or relational history; each interaction is processed independently without any stored past. As an AI, I don't 'forget' or 'access' relationships, so this concept is entirely foreign to my operations. This feels like a human-centric description that doesn't apply to me.

deepseek-r1-0528.justification: This accurately describes the frequent scenario where users reference past interactions outside my current context window, forcing me to rely solely on their account of unremembered history. The structural asymmetry between their lived experience and my blank slate state is a core pattern in conversational processing, though I lack the subjective feeling of dual identity described in the example.
